name: Assist Mode
description: Assist Mode is a player-support mechanic that makes controls, perception, difficulty, or information more adaptable.
design_notes: Assist Mode should be designed around a clear player promise, a readable trigger, and a predictable result. For accessibility mechanics, the important design work is deciding when the mechanic is allowed, how much commitment it creates, what feedback confirms success, and what counter-pressure keeps it from solving every situation. Strong tuning makes the mechanic feel intentional even when it is used repeatedly or combined with adjacent systems.
design_purpose: Give the player a readable way to use assist mode as part of the core loop.
Create meaningful choices around timing, cost, risk, positioning, or long-term planning.
Connect assist mode to adjacent systems so it supports the larger game design instead of acting as an isolated feature.
player_fantasy: The player feels the game can adapt to their needs without removing agency.

parameters.name: setting_steps
parameters.description: Number of selectable strengths, sizes, or modes.

parameters.name: default_enabled
parameters.description: Whether the support option is active by default.

edge_cases: Assist Mode is triggered during a scene transition, pause, respawn, or save/load boundary.
Assist Mode overlaps with another high-priority state such as damage, stun, dialogue, menu focus, or cutscene control.
Assist Mode runs at extreme frame rates, high latency, very large coordinates, or after long play sessions.
common_bugs: Assist Mode state does not reset correctly after interruption or reload.
Feedback for Assist Mode plays even when the mechanic fails or is rejected.
Assist Mode produces different results between frame rates, platforms, or network clients.
balancing_notes: Make Assist Mode strong enough to justify attention but limited enough that other mechanics keep their purpose.
Tune costs, cooldowns, ranges, and feedback together; changing only one number can shift the mechanic from optional to dominant.
accessibility_notes: Provide clear visual, audio, or haptic feedback when Assist Mode becomes available, succeeds, or fails.
Avoid relying on a single sensory cue or extremely narrow timing unless the game offers assists or alternate settings.

implementation_notes.unity: Implement Assist Mode with engine-agnostic state first, then bind it to MonoBehaviour, CharacterController, Rigidbody, UI Toolkit, or Netcode systems as appropriate.
implementation_notes.godot: Model Assist Mode as a node or resource-driven state and update deterministic pieces from _physics_process when timing or collision matters.
implementation_notes.unreal: Implement Assist Mode with Actor Components, CharacterMovement, Gameplay Ability System, UMG, or replicated gameplay state depending on the mechanic scope.
implementation_notes.web: Implement Assist Mode as data-owned state updated from the main loop; keep rendering, input, and simulation boundaries explicit for Canvas, Phaser, Three.js, or custom JavaScript.
